name: English I - Writing Focus
description: A foundational English course that builds core writing skills including paragraph structure, narrative and expository essays, grammar, vocabulary, and literary analysis, culminating in a reflective portfolio.

modules.title: Paragraph Writing
modules.objectives: Understand the structure of a well-developed paragraph including topic sentence, supporting details, and concluding sentence.
modules.readingNote: Read sections on paragraph structure and unity.
modules.videoNote: Watch the introduction to paragraph writing.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the paragraph writing exercises on NoRedInk.
modules.assignment: Write a 5-sentence paragraph about a memorable experience using a clear topic sentence, three supporting details, and a concluding sentence.

modules.title: Narrative Essays
modules.objectives: Craft a narrative essay with a clear beginning, middle, and end using vivid details and a central theme.
modules.readingNote: Read about narrative essay structure and techniques.
modules.videoNote: Watch the overview of narrative essay writing.
modules.practiceNote: Read a sample narrative and answer guided questions.
modules.assignment: Write a 3-paragraph narrative essay about a challenge you faced and what you learned from it.

modules.title: Descriptive Writing
modules.objectives: Use sensory details and figurative language to create vivid descriptions of people, places, and events.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on using sensory details in writing.
modules.assignment: Write a descriptive paragraph about your favorite place using at least three types of sensory details.

modules.title: Expository Essays
modules.objectives: Write clear, informative expository essays that explain a topic using facts, examples, and logical organization.
modules.readingNote: Read about expository essay types and structure.
modules.videoNote: Watch the tutorial on expository essay writing.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the expository writing practice set.
modules.assignment: Write a 4-paragraph expository essay explaining how to accomplish a task or process you know well.

modules.title: Persuasive Writing
modules.objectives: State a clear claim, support it with evidence, and address counterarguments in persuasive writing.
modules.readingNote: Study the structure of persuasive and argumentative essays.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on persuasive writing techniques.
modules.practiceNote: Explore a debate topic and identify pro and con arguments.
modules.assignment: Write a persuasive essay on a school policy issue, taking a clear stance and supporting it with at least two pieces of evidence.

modules.title: Research Basics
modules.objectives: Understand the research process including identifying credible sources, taking notes, and organizing information.
modules.readingNote: Read the overview of the research process.
modules.videoNote: Watch the introduction to academic research.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the research skills module on Khan Academy.
modules.assignment: Choose a topic, find three credible sources, and write a one-page summary of what you found.

modules.title: Citation & MLA
modules.objectives: Properly cite sources using MLA format for in-text citations and works cited pages.
modules.readingNote: Study the MLA general format guidelines.
modules.videoNote: Watch the tutorial on MLA citation format.
modules.practiceNote: Practice creating MLA citations for different source types.
modules.assignment: Create a Works Cited page with five sources in proper MLA format for your previous research assignment.

modules.title: Revision Strategies
modules.objectives: Apply effective revision strategies to improve clarity, organization, and development in written drafts.
modules.readingNote: Read about the revision and proofreading process.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on effective revision strategies.
modules.practiceNote: Use Grammarly to revise and improve a sample paragraph.
modules.assignment: Take one of your previous essays and revise it using the ARMS strategy (Add, Remove, Move, Substitute). Submit both drafts.

modules.title: Grammar Foundations
modules.objectives: Master fundamental grammar rules including parts of speech, subject-verb agreement, and correct punctuation.
modules.readingNote: Review the grammar overview and select three problem areas to study.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the grammar diagnostic and assigned practice exercises.
modules.assignment: Complete a grammar correction exercise with 20 sentences, then write a paragraph demonstrating correct use of five grammar rules.

modules.title: Sentence Variety
modules.objectives: Use a variety of sentence structures including simple, compound, complex, and compound-complex sentences to improve writing style.
modules.readingNote: Study the techniques for creating sentence variety.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on sentence types and variety.
modules.assignment: Rewrite a paragraph using at least four different sentence structures, then label each sentence type.

modules.title: Vocabulary Building
modules.objectives: Expand academic vocabulary through context clues, word roots, and systematic study strategies.
modules.readingNote: Read about strategies for building vocabulary.
modules.videoNote: Watch the vocabulary building strategies video.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the assigned vocabulary lists and practice sessions.
modules.assignment: Create a personal vocabulary journal with 20 new words, including definitions, word roots, and original sentences for each.

modules.title: Reading Comprehension
modules.objectives: Apply active reading strategies to improve understanding, retention, and analysis of written texts.
modules.readingNote: Read about active reading strategies and choose three to practice.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on reading comprehension strategies.
modules.practiceNote: Read two assigned passages and answer the comprehension questions.
modules.assignment: Read a short story and complete a graphic organizer identifying main idea, supporting details, theme, and author's purpose.

modules.title: Literary Analysis Basics
modules.objectives: Identify and analyze basic literary elements including plot, character, setting, theme, and point of view.
modules.readingNote: Read the introduction to writing about literature.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on literary elements and analysis.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the literary analysis guided practice on CommonLit.
modules.assignment: Write a one-page literary analysis of a short story, identifying and explaining three literary elements used by the author.

quizQuestions.question: What is the purpose of a topic sentence in a paragraph?
options: To state the main idea of the paragraph
quizQuestions.answer: To state the main idea of the paragraph
quizQuestions.explanation: A topic sentence introduces the main idea that the rest of the paragraph will support.

quizQuestions.question: Which part of a paragraph directly supports the topic sentence?
quizQuestions.answer: Supporting details
quizQuestions.explanation: Supporting details provide evidence or examples that develop the main idea.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The purpose of a topic sentence in a paragraph is "To state the main idea of the paragraph".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. To state the main idea of the paragraph is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary purpose of a narrative essay?
options: To inform readers about a topic
To persuade readers to take action
To tell a story from the writer's perspective
quizQuestions.answer: To tell a story from the writer's perspective
quizQuestions.explanation: A narrative essay tells a personal story, often sharing an experience and insight gained.

quizQuestions.question: Which element is essential to a strong narrative essay?
quizQuestions.answer: A central conflict or challenge
quizQuestions.explanation: A compelling narrative includes a conflict or challenge that drives the story.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'point of view' refer to in narrative writing?
options: The perspective from which the story is told
quizQuestions.answer: The perspective from which the story is told
quizQuestions.explanation: Point of view determines who is telling the story and how events are perceived.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The primary purpose of a narrative essay is "To tell a story from the writer's perspective".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. To tell a story from the writer's perspective is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What is the main goal of descriptive writing?
options: To create a vivid picture in the reader's mind
quizQuestions.answer: To create a vivid picture in the reader's mind
quizQuestions.explanation: Descriptive writing uses sensory details and figurative language to paint a vivid image.

quizQuestions.question: Which literary device compares two unlike things using 'like' or 'as'?
quizQuestions.answer: Simile
quizQuestions.explanation: A simile is a comparison using 'like' or 'as.'

quizQuestions.question: What are the five senses used in sensory details?
quizQuestions.answer: Sight, sound, smell, taste, touch
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective descriptive writing appeals to all five senses.

quizQuestions.question: Fill in the blank: Complete: In the context of the topic, ___ refers to "which term describes the emotional atmosphere created by descriptive writing."
quizQuestions.answer: Mood
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Mood.

quizQuestions.question: What is the main purpose of an expository essay?
options: To inform and explain a topic clearly
quizQuestions.answer: To inform and explain a topic clearly
quizQuestions.explanation: Expository essays inform or explain a topic using facts and logical organization.

quizQuestions.question: Which is an example of an expository essay type?
quizQuestions.answer: Process (how-to) essay
quizQuestions.explanation: A process essay explains how to do something step by step, a classic expository form.

quizQuestions.question: What should the introduction of an expository essay include?
options: Background information and a thesis statement
quizQuestions.answer: Background information and a thesis statement
quizQuestions.explanation: The introduction provides context and ends with a thesis statement.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'objectivity' mean in expository writing?
quizQuestions.answer: Presenting information without personal bias
quizQuestions.explanation: Objectivity means presenting facts neutrally without personal opinion.

quizQuestions.question: What is a claim in persuasive writing?
options: The writer's main argument or position
quizQuestions.answer: The writer's main argument or position
quizQuestions.explanation: A claim is the central argument the writer makes and defends throughout the essay.

quizQuestions.question: What is a counterargument?
options: An opposing viewpoint that the writer addresses
quizQuestions.answer: An opposing viewpoint that the writer addresses
quizQuestions.explanation: A counterargument presents the opposing side's position, which the writer then refutes.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: A claim in persuasive writing is "The writer's main argument or position".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. The writer's main argument or position is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: A counterargument is "Additional evidence supporting the claim".
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: An opposing viewpoint that the writer addresses.

quizQuestions.question: What is the first step in the research process?
options: Choosing a topic and research question
quizQuestions.answer: Choosing a topic and research question
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective research begins with identifying a focused topic and a clear research question.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is considered a credible source?
quizQuestions.answer: A peer-reviewed academic journal
quizQuestions.explanation: Peer-reviewed journals are evaluated by experts before publication.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'primary source' mean?
options: A source found first in a Google search
An original, firsthand source such as a diary or original research
quizQuestions.answer: An original, firsthand source such as a diary or original research
quizQuestions.explanation: Primary sources are original materials created at the time of study.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The first step in the research process is "Choosing a topic and research question".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Choosing a topic and research question is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What does MLA stand for?
quizQuestions.answer: Modern Language Association
quizQuestions.explanation: MLA stands for Modern Language Association, which publishes style guidelines used in humanities courses.

quizQuestions.question: In MLA format, how are in-text citations typically formatted?
quizQuestions.answer: (Author Page)
quizQuestions.explanation: MLA in-text citations include the author's last name and the page number in parentheses.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "What does MLA stand for" is: Modern Language Association.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Modern Language Association is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What does the 'ARMS' revision strategy stand for?
quizQuestions.answer: Add, Remove, Move, Substitute
quizQuestions.explanation: ARMS guides writers through four revision types: Add, Remove, Move, and Substitute.

quizQuestions.question: What is the difference between revision and proofreading?
options: Revision focuses on content and organization; proofreading focuses on grammar and mechanics
quizQuestions.answer: Revision focuses on content and organization; proofreading focuses on grammar and mechanics
quizQuestions.explanation: Revision addresses big-picture issues; proofreading addresses surface-level errors.

quizQuestions.question: Why is peer review valuable in the revision process?
options: It allows the teacher to grade work
Fresh eyes can catch errors and unclear passages the writer may miss
quizQuestions.answer: Fresh eyes can catch errors and unclear passages the writer may miss
quizQuestions.explanation: Peer reviewers offer an outside perspective that identifies weaknesses the writer cannot see objectively.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "What does the 'ARMS' revision strategy stand for" is: Add, Remove, Move, Substitute.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Add, Remove, Move, Substitute is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What is a sentence fragment?
options: A sentence with too many words
An incomplete sentence that lacks a subject, verb, or complete thought
A sentence that is too formal
quizQuestions.answer: An incomplete sentence that lacks a subject, verb, or complete thought
quizQuestions.explanation: A sentence fragment is missing one or more essential components making it grammatically incomplete.

quizQuestions.question: What is subject-verb agreement?
options: Using the same verb tense throughout
Matching the subject and verb in number (singular/plural)
Placing the subject before the verb
quizQuestions.answer: Matching the subject and verb in number (singular/plural)
quizQuestions.explanation: Subject-verb agreement requires singular subjects use singular verbs and plural subjects use plural verbs.

quizQuestions.question: Which sentence demonstrates correct subject-verb agreement?
quizQuestions.answer: The dogs run fast.
quizQuestions.explanation: 'Dogs' is a plural subject and requires the plural verb 'run.'

quizQuestions.question: What is a simple sentence?
options: A sentence with one independent clause
A sentence with two independent clauses
A sentence with a dependent clause
quizQuestions.answer: A sentence with one independent clause
quizQuestions.explanation: A simple sentence contains one independent clause expressing a complete thought.

quizQuestions.question: What is a compound sentence?
options: A sentence with one subject and multiple verbs
A sentence with two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction or semicolon
A sentence with a dependent clause
quizQuestions.answer: A sentence with two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction or semicolon
quizQuestions.explanation: A compound sentence combines two or more independent clauses using coordinating conjunctions or a semicolon.

quizQuestions.question: What makes a complex sentence different from a compound sentence?
options: A complex sentence contains an independent clause and at least one dependent clause
A complex sentence uses more punctuation
A complex sentence has more subjects
quizQuestions.answer: A complex sentence contains an independent clause and at least one dependent clause
quizQuestions.explanation: Complex sentences combine an independent clause with one or more dependent clauses.

quizQuestions.question: Why is sentence variety important in writing?
options: It creates rhythm and maintains reader interest
It avoids the need for punctuation
It is required by grammar rules
quizQuestions.answer: It creates rhythm and maintains reader interest
quizQuestions.explanation: Varying sentence structures creates rhythm and keeps readers engaged, preventing monotony.

quizQuestions.question: What is a context clue?
options: Information in the surrounding text that helps determine a word's meaning
quizQuestions.answer: Information in the surrounding text that helps determine a word's meaning
quizQuestions.explanation: Context clues are words or sentences near an unfamiliar word that provide hints about its meaning.

quizQuestions.question: What is a word root?
options: A base part of a word from which other words are formed
The last syllable of a word
quizQuestions.answer: A base part of a word from which other words are formed
quizQuestions.explanation: A word root is the core part of a word that carries its fundamental meaning.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: A context clue is "Information in the surrounding text that helps determine a word's meaning".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Information in the surrounding text that helps determine a word's meaning is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: A word root is "The definition of a word".
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: A base part of a word from which other words are formed.

quizQuestions.question: What is the 'main idea' of a passage?
options: The first sentence of the passage
The most important point the author is making
quizQuestions.answer: The most important point the author is making
quizQuestions.explanation: The main idea is the central message that all other details support.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'making an inference' mean in reading?
options: Using clues from the text to draw a conclusion not directly stated
quizQuestions.answer: Using clues from the text to draw a conclusion not directly stated
quizQuestions.explanation: An inference is a logical conclusion drawn from evidence in the text.

quizQuestions.question: What is the author's purpose in a text?
options: The reason the author wrote the text (to inform, persuade, or entertain)
quizQuestions.answer: The reason the author wrote the text (to inform, persuade, or entertain)
quizQuestions.explanation: Author's purpose refers to the writer's reason for creating a text.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The 'main idea' of a passage is "The most important point the author is making".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. The most important point the author is making is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What is a theme in literature?
options: The central message or insight about life conveyed by the work
quizQuestions.answer: The central message or insight about life conveyed by the work
quizQuestions.explanation: Theme is the underlying message or universal truth explored in a literary work.

quizQuestions.question: What is the 'plot' of a story?
options: The lesson learned from the story
The sequence of events that make up the story
The setting where the story takes place
The point of view of the narrator
quizQuestions.answer: The sequence of events that make up the story
quizQuestions.explanation: Plot refers to the series of events following exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, and resolution.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: A theme in literature is "The central message or insight about life conveyed by the work".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. The central message or insight about life conveyed by the work is correct.

questions.question: What are the three essential parts of a paragraph?
options: Topic sentence, supporting details, concluding sentence
questions.answer: Topic sentence, supporting details, concluding sentence
questions.explanation: A well-structured paragraph consists of a topic sentence, supporting details, and a concluding sentence.

questions.question: Which essay type is primarily used to tell a personal story?
questions.answer: Narrative
questions.explanation: A narrative essay tells a personal story from the writer's own experience.

questions.question: What is the purpose of sensory details in descriptive writing?
options: To create vivid images using the five senses
questions.answer: To create vivid images using the five senses
questions.explanation: Sensory details appeal to sight, sound, smell, taste, and touch to create a vivid experience.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "In an expository essay, what is the thesis statement" is: A sentence that states the main point of the essay.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. A sentence that states the main point of the essay is correct.

questions.question: Which appeal relies on emotion to persuade an audience?
questions.answer: Pathos
questions.explanation: Pathos is an emotional appeal that connects with the audience's feelings and sympathies.

questions.question: What does citing a source mean?
options: Giving credit to the original author by referencing their work
questions.answer: Giving credit to the original author by referencing their work
questions.explanation: Citing a source means acknowledging where you found information and giving credit to the original author.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between revision and editing?
options: Revision addresses content and organization; editing fixes grammar and mechanics
questions.answer: Revision addresses content and organization; editing fixes grammar and mechanics
questions.explanation: Revision focuses on improving ideas and structure; editing corrects surface errors.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which sentence correctly demonstrates subject-verb agreement" is: The team are winning..
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: The team is winning..

questions.question: What type of sentence contains one independent clause and one or more dependent clauses?
questions.answer: Complex
questions.explanation: A complex sentence combines one independent clause with at least one dependent clause.

questions.question: What is a context clue?
options: A definition found in the glossary
Information near an unfamiliar word that hints at its meaning
A synonym listed in a thesaurus
questions.answer: Information near an unfamiliar word that hints at its meaning
questions.explanation: Context clues are surrounding words or sentences that help the reader infer meaning.

questions.question: What does MLA in-text citation typically include?
options: Author's last name and page number
questions.answer: Author's last name and page number
questions.explanation: MLA in-text citations are formatted as (LastName PageNumber).

questions.question: True or False: The climax of a narrative is "The turning point or highest point of tension".
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. The turning point or highest point of tension is correct.

questions.question: What does 'inference' mean in reading?
options: Drawing a conclusion from evidence in the text
questions.answer: Drawing a conclusion from evidence in the text
questions.explanation: An inference is a logical conclusion drawn from text evidence combined with prior knowledge.

questions.question: What is personification?
options: Giving human qualities to non-human things
questions.answer: Giving human qualities to non-human things
questions.explanation: Personification gives human characteristics to animals, objects, or ideas.

questions.question: What is a 'credible source' in research?
options: A reliable, trustworthy source with verified information
A source that agrees with your argument
The longest article on the topic
questions.answer: A reliable, trustworthy source with verified information
questions.explanation: A credible source has been reviewed for accuracy and authored by an expert or reputable organization.

questions.question: True or False: The purpose of a writing portfolio is "To display only perfect writing".
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: To show writing growth and a range of skills.

questions.question: What is the theme of a literary work?
options: The central message or universal truth
questions.answer: The central message or universal truth
questions.explanation: Theme is the underlying message about life or human nature the author conveys.

questions.question: What does the ARMS revision strategy stand for?
questions.answer: Add, Remove, Move, Substitute
questions.explanation: ARMS is a revision framework: Add, Remove, Move, and Substitute.

questions.question: What is indirect characterization?
options: The author directly states a character's traits
The character's traits are revealed through actions, dialogue, and reactions
A summary of the character's history
A description of the character's appearance only
questions.answer: The character's traits are revealed through actions, dialogue, and reactions
questions.explanation: Indirect characterization shows personality through behavior, speech, and others' responses.

questions.question: True or False: A hanging indent in MLA format is "The first line is flush left and subsequent lines are indented".
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. The first line is flush left and subsequent lines are indented is correct.

questions.question: Which sentence structure contains two independent clauses and one dependent clause?
questions.answer: Compound-complex
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Compound-complex.

questions.question: What is the author's purpose?
options: The reason the author wrote the text
questions.answer: The reason the author wrote the text
questions.explanation: Author's purpose is the reason behind the writing—commonly to inform, persuade, or entertain.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a run-on sentence?
options: Two independent clauses joined without proper punctuation
A sentence with too many adjectives
A sentence that lacks a subject
questions.answer: Two independent clauses joined without proper punctuation
questions.explanation: A run-on sentence occurs when independent clauses are improperly connected.

questions.question: True or False: The main idea of a text is "The first sentence of the passage".
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: The most important point the author makes.

questions.question: What does 'ethos' mean in persuasive writing?
options: An appeal based on the writer's credibility
questions.answer: An appeal based on the writer's credibility
questions.explanation: Ethos establishes the writer's trustworthiness, expertise, or moral character.

questions.question: What is the difference between a primary and secondary source?
options: Primary sources are online; secondary sources are printed
Primary sources are original materials; secondary sources analyze or interpret primary sources
questions.answer: Primary sources are original materials; secondary sources analyze or interpret primary sources
questions.explanation: Primary sources are firsthand evidence; secondary sources interpret or describe primary sources.

questions.question: What is a symbol in literature?
options: A figure of speech using comparison
An object or element that represents something beyond its literal meaning
questions.answer: An object or element that represents something beyond its literal meaning
questions.explanation: A symbol is a concrete thing that also represents an abstract idea, adding depth to the work.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Why is sentence variety important in writing" is: It creates rhythm and keeps readers engaged.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. It creates rhythm and keeps readers engaged is correct.

questions.question: What is a simile?
options: Giving human traits to non-human things
questions.answer: A comparison using 'like' or 'as'
questions.explanation: A simile explicitly compares two unlike things using the words 'like' or 'as.'

questions.question: What should a self-assessment in a portfolio address?
options: The writer's own strengths, weaknesses, and growth
questions.answer: The writer's own strengths, weaknesses, and growth
questions.explanation: A self-assessment asks writers to reflect critically on their own work.

questions.question: What is the role of a counterargument in persuasive writing?
options: To present opposing views that the writer then refutes
questions.answer: To present opposing views that the writer then refutes
questions.explanation: Including and refuting counterarguments strengthens a persuasive essay.

questions.question: True or False: The exposition in a story's plot is "The climax".
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: The background information introducing characters and setting.

questions.question: What does paraphrasing require the writer to do?
options: Copy the text exactly and add a citation
Restate the information in their own words and cite the source
Only cite the source without restating
Use quotation marks around the original text
questions.answer: Restate the information in their own words and cite the source
questions.explanation: Paraphrasing means expressing another's ideas in your own words while providing a citation.

questions.question: What is the function of a concluding sentence in a paragraph?
options: To restate the main idea and bring the paragraph to a close
questions.answer: To restate the main idea and bring the paragraph to a close
questions.explanation: A concluding sentence signals the end of the paragraph by summarizing or reinforcing the main idea.

questions.question: What distinguishes an expository essay from a persuasive essay?
options: Expository essays inform without taking a personal opinion; persuasive essays argue a position
questions.answer: Expository essays inform without taking a personal opinion; persuasive essays argue a position
questions.explanation: Expository essays present information objectively; persuasive essays advocate for a specific viewpoint.
